Alerts & notifications
Critical — act now
Downtown Funding and Records Search Search BID assessments, DDA grants, TIF reimbursements, board meetings, and financial records in one place.
Annual adopted budget — C.R.S. 29-1-101 et seq · April 10, 2026
Open the search portal →
ADA Title II deadline: April 24, 2026 — 16 days. All local government websites must meet WCAG 2.1 AA. Zero district sites are confirmed compliant on PDF documents.
HB21-1110 · 28 C.F.R. Part 35 · April 8, 2026
Open district audit dashboard →
249 of 284 metro districts have no website. CORA request is the only way a resident can access their own district's records.
EPC Assessor audit · April 2026
See district audit →
WS Districts (WSDM) serves 30+ districts on a single WordPress site. Last calendar entry: 2021. No governance docs. No financials. One compliance letter fixes all 30+ simultaneously.
Platform audit · April 2026
Draft compliance letter →
High priority
All posted PDFs likely fail WCAG 1.3.1. Meeting minutes, budgets, and audit docs are almost certainly untagged. This is the real HB21-1110 failure — not the homepage.
ADA document audit · April 2026
View ADA analysis →
0 of 8 audited sites post their service plan. C.R.S. §32-1-202 requires it. This is the foundational legal document. Not one district links it publicly.
Governance gap · §32-1-202
See audit dashboard →
Annual audit documents: publicly posted by 0 of 8 districts. Required by §32-1-106. Due to State Auditor by July 31. The clock is running.
Governance gap · §32-1-106 · due July 31
See audit dashboard →
Informational
2 CORA requests pending response. Military housing numbers and district governance records both awaiting reply. Response window is still open.
CORA tracker · April 2026
View tracker →
Wolfersberger compliance and Bellamese analysis complete. New findings ready for review on developer agreements, compliance history, and parcel-level records.
Compliance analysis · April 2026
Read analysis →
Jurisdiction finder is live. Enter any El Paso County address to find your metro districts, school district, city jurisdiction, and utilities — plain language, no login.
New · April 2026
Find my jurisdiction →
CSPD Blotter — Weekly History is live. 271 incidents pulled directly from the live blotter feed, paired with a Poisson 95% confidence band to flag statistical anomalies week to week. Tabs are full APG-pattern keyboard accessible; every chart has an aria-label.
New · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open blotter dashboard →
Lorson Ranch financial dashboard is live. Seven cross-pledged metro districts, $94.2M aggregate audited deficit, IGA money flows, and a per-home tax calculator — sourced from 2026 budgets and 2024 audits. Companion plain-language briefing available as a download.
New · Special Districts · April 2026
Open Lorson Ranch dashboard →
CBI Missing Persons form — accessible rebuild is live. Resolves all seven critical/serious findings from the ADA audit: skip link, live regions, focus management, EN/ES localization, real-time validation messaging. Drops into the existing Drupal Webform infrastructure with optional Claude API routing.
New · State · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open CBI form rebuild →
LexisNexis ADAM Program dashboard — accessible rebuild is live. Audit and reference rebuild of the missing-children dashboard: APG-pattern tabs with roving tabindex and full keyboard nav, solid amber focus rings (3:1 against any surface), darker success-green for AA contrast, and "View Poster" placeholders converted from dead anchors to real buttons.
New · Private Sector · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open ADAM dashboard →

If this is an emergency, call 911 immediately.  ·  FBI tip line: 1-800-CALL-FBI  ·  Internet crime → ic3.gov

FBI
Federal Bureau of Investigation Official tip submission portal
secure .gov

Electronic Tip Form

Tell us what you saw.
We handle the rest.

Describe what happened in plain language. You do not need to know which crime category applies. Every submission is reviewed by FBI personnel at headquarters and routed to the right team.

→

Every tip is reviewed by at least two FBI analysts. Categorization happens on our end — not yours. Tips can be submitted anonymously.

Submit a tip

Fields marked * are required. All other fields are optional. You may submit anonymously.

0 / 5000

Be as specific as possible — location, date, names, vehicle descriptions. Do not include your own social security number or financial account numbers.

No Google Maps required. Plain text is fine.

If you know the type of activity — select one (optional — helps routing)

Not sure? Leave blank. Your description is enough.

Internet crimes have a dedicated portal. For faster processing of online scams, fraud, or hacking, you can also file at ic3.gov — run by the FBI. You can still submit here and we'll route it.

Providing your name allows investigators to follow up if needed. It is not required.

Submitted securely over HTTPS.
Reviewed by FBI personnel within 24–48 hours.

✓

Tip received.

Your information has been submitted securely to the FBI's Public Access Center. Every tip is reviewed by at least two analysts.

No further action is required from you.

REF:

Other reporting paths

Internet crime
IC3.gov
Online scams, ransomware, fraud, hacking. FBI-operated dedicated portal.
ic3.gov →
Crimes against children
NCMEC CyberTipline
Child exploitation, missing children. Mandatory reporting for electronic service providers.
missingkids.org →
Disaster fraud
DOJ Disaster Fraud
COVID relief fraud, hurricane aid scams, FEMA fraud. DOJ National Center for Disaster Fraud.
justice.gov →
Local & in-person
FBI Field Offices
56 field offices across the U.S. For time-sensitive situations or if you prefer to speak with someone directly.
Find your office →

The current tips.fbi.gov is a fully JavaScript-rendered Angular application with no server-side fallback. The form shell contains a single <app-root></app-root> tag — if JS fails or loads slowly, citizens see a blank page. It loads Google Maps, Google Tag Manager, and reCAPTCHA before showing anything. The multi-stage crime-type routing forces citizens to self-categorize before submitting — a burden the FBI's own intake process handles internally anyway.

100% client-side JavaScript rendering Architecture
Zero server-rendered content. No-JS fallback absent. Fails WCAG 4.1.1. Screen readers receive an empty document until Angular bootstraps.
Google Maps required for address entry WCAG 1.3.3
Address field depends on the Maps JavaScript API. If the API is slow or blocked, citizens cannot enter a location at all. Sensory characteristic dependency.
Crime-type categorization forced before submission UX / Routing
Citizens must navigate a decision tree to find the right category before they can describe what they saw. FBI's own documentation states every tip is reviewed by two analysts regardless of category — the burden is unnecessary and creates drop-off.
reCAPTCHA v3 loaded on page entry WCAG 1.1.1
Invisible reCAPTCHA tracks users before they've interacted with the form. No accessible alternative provided. Cognitive and privacy barrier for users with assistive technology.
Google Tag Manager / Analytics before consent Privacy / Trust
GTM fires before the user has submitted anything. On a form citizens use to report crimes — often involving personal risk — pre-submission analytics tracking is a trust violation regardless of legality.
Generic system font stack Design
font-family: sans-serif, Arial, Helvetica. No type system, no visual hierarchy investment. A form asking for sensitive civic information should communicate institutional reliability through design.
No visible alternate paths on landing WCAG 2.4.1
IC3.gov, NCMEC, field offices, and disaster fraud — all faster paths for specific crime types — are not surfaced before or alongside the primary form. Citizens who don't know to look elsewhere are routed through the wrong channel.
